text: BRADLEY (LITTLE), a parish in Suffolk, 7½ m. NW of Clare. Area 890 acres. Pop. 33.
BRADLEY-IN-THE-MOORS, a parish in Stafford, 4 m. ESE of Cheadle. Area 570 acres. Pop. 72.
BRADLEY (Lower and Upper), or Bradleys
BOTH, a township in the p. of Kildwick, W. R. of Yorkshire, 2½ m. SSE of Skipton, intersected by the Leeds and Liverpool canal. Area 1,630 acres. Pop. 557.
BRADLEY (NORTH), a parish in Wilts, 2 m. S of Trowbridge. Area 3,020 acres. Pop., with the tything of Southwick, 2,427.
BRADLEY (West), a parish in Somersetshire, 4 m. E by S of Glastonbury. Area 300 acres. Pop., with the hamlet of Panbrook, 116.

BRADLEY, a county of the state of Tennessee, U. S., drained by the Hiawassie and its tributaries. It is mountainous, but on the banks of the streams exhibits considerable fertility. Cap., Cleveland. Pop. in 1840, 7,385, of whom 6,971 were whites, 401 slaves, and 13 free-coloured.—Also a township of Penobscot co., in the state of Maine. Pop. 395.
BRADLEY VALE, a township of Caledonia co., in the state of Vermont, U.S. Pop. in 1840, 50.
BRADLEY'S SHOAL, or Baxos DE LA CANDELARIA, a group of islets in the S Pacific, in the Salomon group, in S lat. 6° 20', and E long. 159° 20'.
BRADMORE, a parish in the co. and 6 m. S of Nottingham. Area 1,560 acres. Pop. 416.

BRADNINCH, or Braines, a borough and parish in the co. of Devon; 163 m. W of London; 8 m. NNE of Exeter. The town is pleasantly situated on an eminence, nearly surrounded by hills, and consists of a long irregular street. The inhabitants are chiefly employed in the manufacture of paper, for which there are here several mills driven by waterpower from the river Culm. Woollen cloths were formerly manufactured here, but this branch of trade has declined considerably; in 1838 there was only one woollen mill here, employing 54 hands. The church is an ancient Gothic structure, containing a richly carved oak screen. Area of p. 4,320 acres. Pop. 1,714.
BRADNOP, a township in the p. of Leek, co. of Stafford, 2 m. ESE of Leek, on the post-road to Ashborne. Pop. 442.
BRADON, a parish, containing the tythings of North and South B., in the co. of Somerset, 3 m. N. by E of Ilminster. Area 530 acres. Pop. 41.
BRADPOLE, a parish in the co. of Dorset, 1 m. NE of Bridport. Area 990 acres. Pop. 1,357.

BRADSBERG, an amt or bailiwick of Norway, in the provinces of Aggerhuus and Christiansand, comprising an area of 126.72 Swedish, or 507 English sq. m.; with a pop. in 1833, of 63,139. It is divided into two fögderi, viz.: Nedra Tellemarken, in the Stift of Aggerhuus, and Ovra-Tellemarken in that of Christiansand; and contains 7 towns, viz.: Skien, Porsgrund, Brevig, Stathelle, Langesund, Osebakken, and Krageröe.

BRADSHAW, a chapelry in the p. of Bolton, co.-palatine of Lancaster, 3 m. NE of Bolton-le- Moors. Area 1,380 acres. Pop. 827. There are several extensive bleaching establishments here. John Bradshaw, who presided at the trial of Charles I., had his family residence at Bradshaw-hall in this neighbourhood.
BRADSHAW-EDGE, a township in the p. of Chapel-in-le-Frith, Derbyshire, 1 m. W of Chapelin-le-Frith. Pop. 1,850.
BRADSTONE, a parish in Devon, 4 m. NE by E of Launceston, on the E bank of the river Tamar. | Area 1,300 acres. Pop. 166.

BRADWELL, a parish in Bucks, 3 m. E by S of Stoney-Stratford, intersected by the London and Birmingham railroad. Area 790 acres. Pop. 381. -Also a township in the p. of Sandbach, 2 m. N by W of Sandbach. Area 1,780 acres. Pop. 344.— Also a hamlet in the p. of Hope, co. of Derby, 31 m. NNE of Tidswell. There are mines of lead and calamine in the vicinity. Pop. 1,273.—Also a parish in Essex, 2 m. W of Coggeshall, on the S bank of the Blackwater river. Area 3,210 acres. Pop. 293. -Also a parish in Suffolk, 3 m. SW of Great Yarmouth. Area 1,540 acres. Pop. 270.

BRADWELL-NEAR-THE-SEA, a parish in Essex, 12 m. E of Maldon; bounded on the N by the estuary of the Blackwater; and on the E by the North sea. Area 3,870 acres. Pop. 1,034. Camden considers this to have been the site of the ancient Saxon city, Ithancestre, and of the Roman station Othona.
BRADWORTHY, a parish in Devon, 61 m. N by W of Holsworthy. Area 12,220 acres. Pop. 1,081.

BRADY, a township of Clearfield co., in the state of Pennsylvania, U. S. Pop. in 1840, 692.—Also a township of Williams co., in the state of Ohio, on the Tiffens. Pop. 351.—Also a township of Kalamazoo, in the state of Michigan, 146 m. W of Detroit. Pop. 1,175.
BRADY (FORT), a fort in the North-West Territory, U. S., on the St. Mary, a little to the SE of the Falls.
BRAEMAR, a district, intersected by the Grampian chain, in the SW extremity of Aberdeenshire.

BRAFFERTON, a township in the p. of Great Aycliff, co.-palatine of Durham, 43 m. N by E of Darlington, intersected by the Great North of England railway. Area 2,080 acres. Pop. 211. - Also a parish, comprising the townships of B., Thorntonbridge, and Helperby, within the liberty of St. Peter of York, but locally in the wapentake of Bulmer, N. R. of Yorkshire, 4 m. NE of Boroughbridge. B. and Helperby are apparently but one village, occupying opposite sides of the same street. Area of p. 4,860 acres. Pop. 873. Area of t. 1,990 acres. Pop. 179.
BRAFFIELD-ON-THE-GREEN, a parish in Northamptonshire, 5 m. E by S of Northampton. Area 1,980 acres. Pop. 428.

BRAGA, or Bracara, a town of Portugal, the cap. of the prov. of Minho, and of the comarca of the same name, situated on an eminence in a broad open valley, between the Cavado to the N, and the Rio d'Este to the S; at an alt. of 620 ft. above sea-level; 33 m. NNE of Oporto; and 210 m. NNE of Lisbon. Pop. 15,000. It is surrounded by ancient walls, flanked with towers; and is defended by a castle of great strength. The streets are broad and regular; and there are seven public squares adorned with fountains. The principal buildings are the cathedral, a magnificent edifice, rebuilt in the 12th cent. by Count Henry, and containing his tomb; the archiepiscopal palace and library, the parish churches, of which there are six, the episcopal seminary, the college, the hospital, alms house, &c. B. possesses manufactories of cloth and other woollen fabrics, linen, calico, beaver hats, jewellery, iron-ware, fire-arms, and cutlery, and wax bleacheries. Two cattle fairs are held here annually.—This town, the Bracara Augusta of the Romans, is of great antiquity; and it still retains extensive remains of its ancient importance, including a temple, an amphitheatre, an aqueduct, &c. It is famous for the councils held here in the 5th and 6th cent.; and it is still the see of the pri- BRA and it afterwards successively passed under the dominion of the Visigoths and the Arabs. On a hill, a little to the E of the town, is the famous Sanctuario do bom Jesus do Monte. The view from the summit of this hill is magnificent.
BRAGADO, a lake of La Plata, in the prov. of Buenos Ayres, in S lat. 35° 10′, and W long. 60° 38′. It discharges itself into the Salado.

BRAGANÇA, or Braganza, a town of Portugal, the cap. of the comarca of the same name, in the prov. of Tras-os-Montes, on the r. bank of the Fervanza, an affluent of the Sabor; 25 m. NW of Miranda; and 78 m. NE of Lamego. Pop. 4,000. It is partly enclosed by walls, now greatly dilapidated; and is defended by a strong citadel. It is the see of a bishop; and contains 2 churches and a college. Taffeta and velvet are the chief articles of manufacture.—B. was erected into a duchy in 1442 by Alphonso V.; and, in 1640, its eighth duke, John II., became king of Portugal under the title of John IV. The immediately adjacent territory is flat and fertile; but the comarca is generally mountainous. It comprises 124 parishes, with a pop. in 1836 of 114,501.

BRAGANÇA, a town and port of Brazil, formerly called Cayte or Caité, in the prov. and 95 m. ENE of Para, on the l. bank of the Caité, 15 m. from the entrance of that river into the Atlantic, in S lat. 1° 30'. Pop. in 1840, 6,000. It is situated in a plain, and consists of two parts united by a bridge. It is one of the oldest and most flourishing cities in the prov. The comarca of B., comprising the districts of B. and Turiacu, had a pop. of 9,782 in 1840.—Also a town of Brazil, in the prov. and 50 m. N of Sao-Paulo, near the l. bank of the Piracicaba, founded in 1797. The pop. of this town and its environs was estimated, in 1840, at 10,000. Pigs are extensively reared in the district for the supply of Rio-de-Janeiro.—Also a village in the prov. of Espirito-Santo, 20 m. E of Vianna, between the rivers Pardo and Santa-Maria.
BRAGANCA NOVA. See AVEIRO.
BRAGUN, a village of Russia, in the gov. of Circassia, on the Sonjah, near the confluence of that river with the Terek, and 25 m. WNW of Aksai. It is the residence of a prince of the Koumuk tribe.

BRAHE, or Braa, a river of Prussia, which takes its rise in the regency of Köslin in Pomerania, to the NE of Rummelsburg; passes Zieten, Rittel, and Koronowa, in the prov. of W Prussia; and unites at Bromberg with the Vistula. It is connected, also, by the Bromberg canal, with the Netze. In the upper part of its course it expands into several lakes, of which the principal are those of Zieten and Muskendorf.

BRAHE, or Brahetrolleborg, a village of Denmark, cap. of a barony of the same name, in the island of Fyen, or Funen, 13 m. WNW of Svendborg. It contains a fine church, belonging to the ancient Bernardine convent, founded in the 12th cent.
BRAHEMAN, a town of Afghanistan, in the dist. of Sewestan, 40 m. SE of Sira Killa, and 165 m. SSE of Kandahar.

BRAHESTAD, a town of Russia in Europe, in the grand duchy of Finland, in the gov. and 40 m. SW of Uleaborg, on a bay of the gulf of Bothnia. Pop. 1,160. It has a port; but large vessels are obliged to anchor at the distance of 3 m. from the town. Pitch, tar, butter, fish, especially salmon, deals, and tallow, form its chief articles of commerce.—This town was founded in 1649 by Count Brahe, from whom it derived its name. BRAHILOV, BRAILA, BRAILOFF, IBRAHIL, OF

IBRAÏLA, a town of Turkey in Europe, the cap. of a dist. of the same name, in Lower Wallachia, on the 1. bank of the Danube, opposite Matzini; 13 m. SSW mate of Portugal. The Suevi made it their capital; of Galatz, and 100 m. ENE of Bukarest. It is politically included in the sanjak of Silistria in Bulgaria. Pop. about 8,000. The town is defended by a strong castle. Its port, formed by a branch of the Danube, is accessible to the largest vessels which navigate that river; the depth between B. and Salina being nowhere less than 18 ft.; and it is the chief emporium of the foreign trade of the principality. In 1834 it was entered by 185 vessels from the Black sea. In 1835 there arrived at B. 14 British ships, 12 with assorted cargoes, and 3 in ballast; but in 1838 of 968 vessels which loaded here only 6 were British. Defended by a small island from the ice drifted down in spring, vessels can winter at B. in perfect security; and the operations of lading and unlading are carried on here with facility, all the warehouses being on the banks of the river. Its principal exports are grain, chiefly wheat, tallow, hides, wool, salt, timber, and other productions of the soil. In 1838 the exports in grain amounted to 61,524 quarters wheat; 37,200 q. Indian corn; and 106,230 q. barley: the total value being £87,580. The exports in tallow amounted, in 1837, to 18,112 cwt.; in 1848 to 27,557 cwt. The total exports in 1837 amounted to £113,481; in 1838 to £148,238. Cheese, kidney-beans, linseed, and wool, form the articles of export next in importance to grain and tallow. The merchandise imported into B. in transit up the Danube, in 1837, was officially valued at £10,731, sugar, coffee, and oil, forming the principal items. Extensive fisheries of sturgeon are carried on in the river. In 1770 B. was twice taken, and, to a great extent, destroyed by the Russians.—The district of B. is flat and marshy, but extremely fertile. It is watered on the N by the Sereth; on the E by the Danube; and on the S by the Kamasu.
BRAHMAPUTRA, commonly written Burram-

POOTER, a mighty river of Eastern India, exceeding the Ganges both in size and in length of course. Of its exact source we are yet ignorant, although we possess many hypotheses upon the subject. For many years this river, on the authority of Major Rennell and Captain Turner, was supposed to be a continuation of the Sanpu of Tibet; but recent discoveries have tended to bring this conjecture into disrepute, at least so far as refers to the eastern branch, or Lohit, the course of which has been traced until it diminishes to a shallow rocky stream, broken by rapids, among the range of mountains bounding Assam to the NE. It has also been ascertained that Brahmacund is not its source. Some geographers, however, expect that a connexion between the B. and Sanpu will still be established by means of the Dihong, or western branch; but this appears improbable, for a mountainous chain-apparently a prolongation of the Himalaya—seems to extend without interruption eastward far beyond the long. of the Dihong. Having quitted Assam, which it divides into two parts, the B. enters Bengal, in the district of Rangamatty; and then turning S, runs 300 m. direct distance more, through Bengal, till it joins the Ganges, below Luckipore, within 30 m. of the sea, after a comparative course of at least 1,600 m. In its passage through the long valley of Assam, it receives 34 streams from the Himalaya mountains on the N, and 24 from the Garrow mountains on the S, which are all navigable at all seasons for boats. After entering Bengal, it is augmented by a number of tributary streams from the Butan and Tibetian mountains. For the last 60 m. of its course, it is from 4 to 5 m. wide; and below Luckipore its channel is expanded to the breadth of 10 m. The doab between the B. and the Ganges is soft alluvial soil; and in 1809, with a little more flood, the B. would have probably swept 30,000 sq. m. of rich land into the sea.—See Assam, p. 392; and BENGAL, p. 681. BRA
BRAHUICK MOUNTAINS. See BELUCHISTAN. P. 670.
BRAHUIS. See Beluchistan. P. 672.
BRAID HILLS, a range of low cultivated hills, an offshoot of the Pentland range, 2 m. S of Edin burgh. Their most elevated point has an alt. of 700 ft. above sea-level.
BRAIL, a town of Switzerland, in the cant. of Grisons, on the l. bank of the Inn, 30 m. ESE of Chur.
BRAILA. See Brahilov.

BRAILES, a parish in Warwickshire, 4½ m. E by S of Shipston-on-Stour. Area 5,220 acres. Pop. 1,284. BRAILOF, RRAILOV, or BRAHILOF, a town of Russia, in the gov. of Podolia, in the dist. and 12 m. SSW of Vinnitsa, on the r. bank of the Rov, near the confluence of that river with the Bug. Pop. 1,000.
BRAILSFORD, a parish in Derbyshire, 7 m. NW by W of Derby. Area 5,200 acres. Pop., with the hamlet of Ednaston, 756.
BRAIN, a commune of France, in the dep. of Ille-et-Vilaine, cant. and 12 m. ENE of Redon, on the r. bank of the Vilaine. Pop. 2,112.
BRAINARD'S BRIDGE, a village in Rensselaer co., in the state of New York, U. S., 16 m. NE of Albany. Pop. in 1840, 200.

BRAINE-L'ALLEUD, or Braine-La-Leude, a commune and town of Belgium, in the prov. of S Brabant, 6 m. NNE of Nivelles, and 3 m. SSW of Waterloo, on an affluent of the Seine. Pop. 3,200. This com. formed a portion of the scene of the operations of the battle of Waterloo. It contains manufactories of common cloth, glass, starch, a cotton spinning-mill, a tannery, and a salt-work.

BRAINE-LE-COMTE, a commune and town of Belgium, the cap. of the cant. of the same name, in the prov. of Hainault, 13 m. NNE of Mons. Pop. in 1847, 5,183. It contains cotton spinning-mills, dyeworks, and lace manufactories. In the environs are quarries of freestone. At this place the railway Du Midi bifurcates,—one branch going to Charleroi and Namur, and the other to Mons.
BRAINERD, a village and missionary station in Hamilton co., in the state of Tennessee, U. S., 60 m. SSW of Washington, on the Little Chickamaugah creek, which is here navigable.
BRAINS, a commune of France, in the dep. of the Loire-Inferieure, cant. of Bouaye. Pop. 1,019.— Also a commune in the dep. of the Sarthe, cant. of Loué, 12 m. W of Le Mans. Pop. 1,207.
BRAIN-SUR-ALLONES, a commune of France, in the dep. of the Maine-et-Loire, cant. and 9 m. NE of Saumur. Pop. 1,565.
BRAIN-SUR-L'AUTHION, a commune of France, in the dep. of the Maine-et-Loire, cant. of Angers. Pop. 1,596. The environs afford good white wine.
BRAIN-SUR-LONGUENE'E, a commune of France, in the dep. of the Maine-et-Loire, cant. of Le Lion-d'Angers. Pop. 1,068.
BRAINTFIELD, or Bramfield, a parish within the liberty of St. Albans, but locally situated in Hertfordshire, 33 m. NW of Hertford.

BRAINTREE, a market-town and parish in Essex, 40 m. NE of London, 8 m. E of Dunmow. Area 2,500 acres. Pop. 3,670. The town of B. is divided from the village of Bocking by the turnpike road. It is one of the polling-places for the members for North Essex. In 1838 there was a silk-mill here, employing 106 hands. Silk-throwing was introduced in 1810; and some crapes are here manufactured by the hand-loom for the London dealers. The number of weavers in Braintree, Bocking, and the vicinity, in 1838, was about 700. Straw-plaiting has been recently introduced. The manufacture of woollen cloth-introduced into B. in the reign of Queen Elizabeth by some Flemings who had fled to England to avoid the of Launceston, on the E bank of the river Tamar. Area 1,300 acres. Pop. 166.

BRADWELL, a parish in Bucks, 3 m. E by S of Stoney-Stratford, intersected by the London and Birmingham railroad. Area 790 acres. Pop. 381. -Also a township in the p. of Sandbach, 2 m. N by W of Sandbach. Area 1,780 acres. Pop. 344.— Also a hamlet in the p. of Hope, co. of Derby, 31 m. NNE of Tidswell. There are mines of lead and calamine in the vicinity. Pop. 1,273.—Also a parish in Essex, 2 m. W of Coggeshall, on the S bank of the Blackwater river. Area 3,210 acres. Pop. 293. -Also a parish in Suffolk, 3 m. SW of Great Yarmouth. Area 1,540 acres. Pop. 270.

BRADWELL-NEAR-THE-SEA, a parish in Essex, 12 m. E of Maldon; bounded on the N by the estuary of the Blackwater; and on the E by the North sea. Area 3,870 acres. Pop. 1,034. Camden considers this to have been the site of the ancient Saxon city, Ithancestre, and of the Roman station Othona.
BRADWORTHY, a parish in Devon, 61 m. N by W of Holsworthy. Area 12,220 acres. Pop. 1,081.

BRADY, a township of Clearfield co., in the state of Pennsylvania, U. S. Pop. in 1840, 692.—Also a township of Williams co., in the state of Ohio, on the Tiffens. Pop. 351.—Also a township of Kalamazoo, in the state of Michigan, 146 m. W of Detroit. Pop. 1,175.
BRADY (FORT), a fort in the North-West Territory, U. S., on the St. Mary, a little to the SE of the Falls.
BRAEMAR, a district, intersected by the Grampian chain, in the SW extremity of Aberdeenshire.

BRAFFERTON, a township in the p. of Great Aycliff, co.-palatine of Durham, 4 m. N by E of Darlington, intersected by the Great North of England railway. Area 2,080 acres. Pop. 211. - Also a parish, comprising the townships of B., Thorntonbridge, and Helperby, within the liberty of St. Peter of York, but locally in the wapentake of Bulmer, N. R. of Yorkshire, 4 m. NE of Boroughbridge. B. and Helperby are apparently but one village, occupying opposite sides of the same street. Area of p. 4,860 acres. Pop. 873. Area of t. 1,990 acres. Pop. 179.
BRAFFIELD-ON-THE-GREEN, a parish in Northamptonshire, 5 m. E by S of Northampton. Area 1,980 acres. Pop. 428.

BRAGA, or Bracara, a town of Portugal, the cap. of the prov. of Minho, and of the comarca of the same name, situated on an eminence in a broad open valley, between the Cavado to the N, and the Rio d'Este to the S; at an alt. of 620 ft. above sea-level; 33 m. NNE of Oporto; and 210 m. NNE of Lisbon. Pop. 15,000. It is surrounded by ancient walls, flanked with towers; and is defended by a castle of great strength. The streets are broad and regular; and there are seven public squares adorned with fountains. Two cattle fairs are held here annually.—This town, the Bracara Augusta of the Romans, is of great antiquity; and it still retains extensive remains of its ancient importance, including a temple, an amphitheatre, an aqueduct, &c. It is famous for the councils held here in the 5th and 6th cent.; and it is still the see of the primate of Portugal. The Suevi made it their capital; BRA and it afterwards successively passed under the dominion of the Visigoths and the Arabs. On a hill, a little to the E of the town, is the famous Sanctuario do bom Jesus do Monte. The view from the summit of this hill is magnificent.

BRAHMAPUTRA, commonly written Burram-

POOTER, a mighty river of Eastern India, exceeding the Ganges both in size and in length of course. Of its exact source we are yet ignorant, although we possess many hypotheses upon the subject. For many years this river, on the authority of Major Rennell and Captain Turner, was supposed to be a continuation of the Sanpu of Tibet; but recent discoveries have tended to bring this conjecture into disrepute, at least so far as refers to the eastern branch, or Lohit, the course of which has been traced until it diminishes to a shallow rocky stream, broken by rapids, among the range of mountains bounding Assam to the NE. It has also been ascertained that Brahmacund is not its source. Some geographers, however, expect that a connexion between the B. and Sanpu will still be established by means of the Dihong, or western branch; but this appears improbable, for a mountainous chain-apparently a prolongation of the Himalaya—seems to extend without interruption eastward far beyond the long. of the Dihong. Having quitted Assam, which it divides into two parts, the B. enters Bengal, in the district of Rangamatty; and then turning S, runs 300 m. direct distance more, through Bengal, till it joins the Ganges, below Luckipore, within 30 m. of the sea, after a comparative course of at least 1,600 m. In its passage through the long valley of Assam, it receives 34 streams from the Himalaya mountains on the N, and 24 from the Garrow mountains on the S, which are all navigable at all seasons for boats. After entering Bengal, it is augmented by a number of tributary streams from the Butan and Tibetian mountains. For the last 60 m. of its course, it is from 4 to 5 m. wide; and below Luckipore its channel is expanded to the breadth of 10 m. The doab between the B. and the Ganges is soft alluvial soil; and in 1809, with a little more flood, the B. would have probably swept 30,000 sq. m. of rich land into the sea.—See Assam, p. 392; and BENGAL, p. 681. BRA
BRAHUICK MOUNTAINS. See BELUCHISTAN. P. 670.
BRAHUIS. See Beluchistan. P. 672.
BRAID HILLS, a range of low cultivated hills, an offshoot of the Pentland range, 2 m. S of Edin burgh. Their most elevated point has an alt. of 700 ft. above sea-level.
BRAIL, a town of Switzerland, in the cant. of Grisons, on the l. bank of the Inn, 30 m. ESE of Chur.
BRAILA. See BRAHILOV.

BRAILES, a parish in Warwickshire, 45 m. E by S of Shipston-on-Stour. Area 5,220 acres. Pop. 1,284. BRAILOF, RRAÏLOV, or BRAHILOF, a town of Russia, in the gov. of Podolia, in the dist. and 12 m. SSW of Vinnitsa, on the r. bank of the Rov, near the confluence of that river with the Bug. Pop. 1,000.
BRAILSFORD, a parish in Derbyshire, 7 m. NW by W of Derby. Area 5,200 acres. Pop., with the hamlet of Ednaston, 756.
BRAIN, a commune of France, in the dep. of Ille-et-Vilaine, cant. and 12 m. ENE of Redon, on the r. bank of the Vilaine. Pop. 2,112.
BRAINARD'S BRIDGE, a village in Rensselaer co., in the state of New York, U. S., 16 m. NE of Albany. Pop. in 1840, 200.

BRAINE-L'ALLEUD, or Braine-La-Leude, a commune and town of Belgium, in the prov. of S Brabant, 6 m. NNE of Nivelles, and 3 m. SSW of Waterloo, on an affluent of the Seine. Pop. 3,200. This com. formed a portion of the scene of the operations of the battle of Waterloo. It contains manufactories of common cloth, glass, starch, a cotton spinning-mill, a tannery, and a salt-work.

BRAINE-LE-COMTE, a commune and town of Belgium, the cap. of the cant. of the same name, in the prov. of Hainault, 13 m. NNE of Mons. Pop. in 1847, 5,183. It contains cotton spinning-mills, dyeworks, and lace manufactories. In the environs are quarries of freestone. At this place the railway Du Midi bifurcates,—one branch going to Charleroi and Namur, and the other to Mons.
BRAINERD, a village and missionary station in Hamilton co., in the state of Tennessee, U. S., 60 m. SSW of Washington, on the Little Chickamaugah creek, which is here navigable.
BRAINS, a commune of France, in the dep. of the Loire-Inferieure, cant. of Bouaye. Pop. 1,019.— Also a commune in the dep. of the Sarthe, cant. of Loué, 12 m. W of Le Mans. Pop. 1,207.
BRAIN-SUR-ALLONES, a commune of France, in the dep. of the Maine-et-Loire, cant. and 9 m. NE of Saumur. Pop. 1,565.
BRAIN-SUR-L'AUTHION, a commune of France, in the dep. of the Maine-et-Loire, cant. of Angers. Pop. 1,596. The environs afford good white wine.
BRAIN-SUR-LONGUENE'E, a commune of France, in the dep. of the Maine-et-Loire, cant. of Le Lion-d'Angers. Pop. 1,068.
BRAINTFIELD, or Bramfield, a parish within the liberty of St. Albans, but locally situated in Hertfordshire, 3\frac{1}{2} m. NW of Hertford.

BRAINTREE, a market-town and parish in Essex, 40 m. NE of London, 8 m. E of Dunmow. Area 2,500 acres. Pop. 3,670. The town of B. is divided from the village of Bocking by the turnpike road. It is one of the polling-places for the members for North Essex. In 1838 there was a silk-mill here, employing 106 hands. Silk-throwing was introduced in 1810; and some crapes are here manufactured by the hand-loom for the London dealers. The number of weavers in Braintree, Bocking, and the vicinity, in 1838, was about 700. Straw-plaiting has been recently introduced. The manufacture of woollen cloth-introduced into B. in the reign of Queen Elizabeth by some Flemings who had fled to England to avoid the persecution of the Duke of Alva-has declined .- The B. poor-law union comprehends 14 parishes, embracing an area of 65 sq. m., with a population returned in 1831 at 15,097; in 1841, at 16,018. The average annual expenditure on the poor of this district, during the three years preceding the formation of the union, was £13,448. Expenditure in 1838, £9,495; in 1846, £9,149.

BRAINTREE, a township of Orange co., in the state of Vermont, U. S., 23 m. S of Montpelier. It is watered by branches of the White river, and possesses fine pasturage. Pop. in 1840, 1,232.—Also a township of Norfolk co., in the state of Massachusetts, U. S., 14 m. S of Boston. Pop. 2,168. It is hilly, and the soil, which consists of gravelly loam, is generally fertile. The Mantiquot, by which it is intersected, affords good water-power. It has considerable manufactures, and some shipping employed in the coasting-trade and fisheries. A fine species of granite is obtained here.

BRAINTREM, a township of Lucerne co., in the state of Pennsylvania, U. S., bounded on the S by the Susquehanna, 163 m. NNW of Harrisburg. Pop. in 1840, 1,355. The surface is hilly, and the soil gravelly alluvium.
BRAISEWORTH, or Breisworth, a parish in Suffolk, 2 m. SSW of Eye, intersected by the Eastern Counties railroad. Area 1,400 acres. Pop. 151.

BRAISNE, a canton, commune, and town of France, in the dep. of Aisne, arrond. of Soissons.— The cant. comprises 42 com., and in 1831 contained a pop. of 12,685.—The town—sometimes called Braisne-sur-Vesle—is situated in a fine plain, on the r. bank of the Vesle, 10 m. ESE of Soissons, and 17 m. S of Laon. Pop. 1,352. In the vicinity is a cold mineral spring.
BRAITHWAITE, a township in the p. of Crossthwaite, in Cumberland, 2 m. W of Keswick. Pop. in 1801, 282; in 1841, 383.
BRAITHWELL, a parish and township in the W. R. of Yorkshire, 7 m. SW by S of Doncaster. Area 2,990 acres. Pop. of p. 800; of t. 447.
BRAIX (St.), a town of Switzerland, in the cant. of Berne, 8 m. SSE of Porrentruy.

BRAK, or BARAK, a river of India beyond the Ganges, which takes its rise in the prov. of Munipore; runs, in a circuitous course, through the districts of Cachar and Silhet; and unites with the Surmak, 11 m. NE of the confluence of that river with the Brahmaputra.
BRAKAUR, a lake of Sweden, in the laen of Umea, formed by an expansion of the Umea.
BRAKE, a town in the principality of Lippe-Detmold, 11 m. Sof Lemgo. It gives its name to a bailiwick of which Lemgo is the cap.

BRAKEL, a town of Prussia, cap. of a circle of the same name, in the prov. of Westphalia, regency and 42 m. SSE of Minden, and 18 m. E of Paderborn, on the Brucht, 2 m. above its confluence with the Nette. Pop. 2,533. It contains a parish-church a synagogue, an hospital, and an alms-house. It has also manufactories of linen, tobacco, and glass. In the environs are some mineral springs.—The circle comprises an area of 57 sq. m., and has a pop. of 20,861. It produces rye, lint, and hops.
BRAKEL (NEDER), a commune and town of Belgium, in the prov. of E Flanders, 8 m. ESE of Outhe Scheldt. Pop. 3,807. It contains numerous breweries, malt-mills, and oil-presses.
BRAKEL (OP), a commune and village of Belgium, in the prov. of E Flanders, a mile SSW of Brakel Neder.

BRAKE (THE), or SMALL DOWNS, an anchorage of considerable extent lying between the Brake sand BRA and the shore N of Deal, in Kent. The B. sand is about 5 m. in length, with a depth upon it at low water spring-tides of only from 3 to 12 ft. Sir John Rennie has proposed a plan for converting the Small Downs into a close harbour, by constructing a solid work along the spine of the B. sand, 5 m. in length, and to be brought up 2 ft. above high water-mark. The cost of this gigantic work he estimates at £3,280,000.

BRAKKE, a river of S Africa, in the Cape territory, in the district of Somerset, which runs SSE, passes Brakke river Fountain, and unites with the Little Fish river, 12 m. W of its junction with the Great Fish river.—Also a river in the district of Uitenhage, which falls into the Zwartkops 11 m. ESE of Uitenhage.
BRAKKE FOUNTAIN, a village of S Africa, in the Cape territory, in the district and 55 m. NNE of Clanwilliam.—Also a v. in the district and 22 m. SW of Graaff Reynet.

BRAKKE, or BRAAK (GREAT), a river of S Africa, in the Cape territory, which takes its rise in the Zuure Berg, runs along the confines of the districts of Somerset and Graaff Reynet, and unites with the Great Fish river 42 m. NE of Graaff Reynet.
BRAKKE (LITTLE), a river of S Africa, in the district of Somerset, which falls into the Great B., 8 m. N of the junction of that river with the Great Fish river.

BRAKNAS, or EBRAKNAS, a Moorish tribe who inhabit the confines of the SW extremity of the Sahara, near the N bank of the Senegal. Their territory, according to Caillie, is situated 60 leagues ENE of St. Louis; and is bounded on the N by the territory of the Oulad Lame; on the NE by that of the Komts; on the E by that of the Douiches; on the S by the Senegal. They carry on an extensive commerce in gum, the produce of the great gum-forests of the district, which they exchange for guns and cloth. When the rainy season commences, that is, at the beginning of August, they return to the NE, where they find plentiful pasturage at this season.—Braknas or Escale du Cog, their cap., is on the r. bank of the Senegal, 5 m. NW of Fort Podor. Caillie has given a full account of these Moors in his Travels through Central Africa.
BRALA PULO, a small island of the Chinese sea, near the E coast of the Malay peninsula, in N lat. 4° 48′, E long. 103° 40′.

BRALIN, a town of Prussia, in the prov. of Silesia, regency and 40 m. ENE of Breslau, and in the circle and 9 m. E of Wartenberg. Pop. 1,000. It contains a castle, custom-house, two churches, and an alms-house, and has manufactories of linen. The commerce consists chiefly in cattle.
BRALO, a town of Greece, in the prov. of Livadia, 18 m. NNE of Salona.
BRASVILLE, a village of Monroe co., in the state of Ohio, U. S. Pop. in 1840, 194.

BRAM, a commune of France, in the dep. of the Aude, cant. of Fanjeaux, on the Preuille, and near the Canal du Midi, on which it has a small port, 12 m. SE of Castelnaudary. Pop. 1,425. It contains a fine castle. The environs are very fertile.—Also a river of Austria, in the circle of Upper Austria, which falls into the Inn opposite Scharding.—Also a river of Holstein, which runs in a W direction, and passing Bramstedt, unites with the Stor 8 m. WNW of that town.

BRAMADEO, or Brahma - Deva, a celebrated Hindu temple, in the prov. of Delhi, 75 m. NE of Bareily. It is situated in the Great Saul forest, on the Goggra, which here descends in a magnificent denarde, and 16 m. SSE of Ghent, on an affluent of cataract from the Kumaon mountains, through a steep and narrow defile. A stone casement, surrounding a sacred peepul tree, forms the temple, and is now the only object which distinguishes the locality.
BRAMANS, or BRAMANT, a town of Savoy, in the prov. of Maurienne, on the l. bank of the Arc, 22 m. ESE of St. Jean de Maurienne.

BRAMBACH, or Prombach, a town of Saxony, in the circle and 35 m. S of Zwickau, in the bailiwick of Voigtsberg, 7 m. SSE of Adorf, and the same distance ESE of Asch, in a gorge of the Erzgebirge, and near the Bohemian frontier. Pop. in 1834, 1,508. There is a custom-house here.

BRAMBANA, or Probolingo, a village in the island of Java, in the district of Mataram, near the S bank of the Salo, 3 m. NNE of Yugyacarta, and 7 m. SW of Suracarta. It is remarkable for the number and extent of the ruins which it contains. Of these the most extraordinary is the group known as 'the Thousand temples,' which covers an area 600 ft. in length, and 550 ft. in breadth. It consists of 4 rows of small pyramidical temples, regularly built of blocks of hewn stone, profusely sculptured, and each containing an image of Buddha. In the centre of the square is an edifice 60 ft. in height, divided into several compartments, and enclosing a variety of representations of the idol Siva. The entrances to the group face the cardinal points of the compass; and each is guarded by colossal warders, in a kneeling posture. The date of these structures is supposed to be about the close of the 12th, and the commencement of the 13th cent. The state which existed at B. flourished about the years 1266 and 1296; but of this state, says Crawfurd, "we know not one syllable of the real history." A full account of the ancient remains at B., from a journal kept by Lieut. Col. Mackenzie, will be found in the 2d vol. of the Asiatic Journal, 1816.

BRAMBER, a parish in Sussex, 3 m. SE of Steyning. Area 870 acres. Pop. 138. The v., which now consists of a few cottages, was formerly of some importance, having returned two members to parliament from the 7° Edward IV. till 1832, when it was disfranchised.

BRAMBERG, a village of Bavaria, in the circle of Unter-Franken-Aschaffenburg, 20 m. from Hassfurt. Pop. 200. In the vicinity are the ruins of the ancient castle which occupied the highest summit of the Hassgebirge.-Also a v. of the archduchy of Austria, in the circle of Saltzburg, 14 m. SSW of Schwatz, and 19 m. S of Rattenberg. It contains manufactories of vitriol; and in the vicinity are mines of copper and sulphur.
BRAMCOTE, a parish in the co. of Nottingham, 44 m. WSW of Nottingham. Area 1,000 acres. Pop. 732.
BRAMDEAN, a parish in the co. of Southampton, 4 m. S by E of New Alresford. Area 1,360 acres. Pop. 225.
BRAMERAU, a town of Holstein, on a tributary of the Eyder, 16 m. WSW of Kiel.
BRAMERTON, a parish in Norfolk, 5 m. SE of Norwich. Area 1,000 acres. Pop. 229.
BRAMFIELD, a parish in Suffolk, 2 m. S of Halesworth. Area 2,050 acres. Pop. 746.
BRAMFORD, a parish in Suffolk, 3 m. NW of Ipswich. Area 1,940 acres. Pop. 881.
BRAMHALL, or Bramall, a township in the p. of Stockport, Cheshire, 3 m. S of Stockport. Area 3,250 acres. Pop. 1,396.

BRAMHAM, a parish in the W. R. of Yorkshire, 4 m. SSE of Wetherby. It comprises the townships of B. and Clifford. Area 5,260 acres. Pop. 2,760. In the year 1408, Sir Thomas Rokeby, sheriff of Yorkshire, fought and defeated the earl of Northumberland here, thereby securing the crown to Henry IV. On Bramham-moor are large remains of the Roman way called Watling-street.
BRAMHOPE, a township in the p. of Otley, W. R. of Yorkshire, 4 m. ESE of Otley. Area 1,290 acres. Pop. 350.

BRAMINI, or BAMINI, a river of Hindostan, which takes its rise in the S part of the prov. of extent. There are also extensive bleaching-fields .- ~ BRA Bahar, in the dist. of Burwa; waters the E side of the prov. of Gundwana, under the name of the Soank; crosses the prov. of Orissa, and falls into the bay of Bengal, near Cape Palmyras. In the dist. of Kuttack-in which it takes the appellation of the Bernrah—it divides into several branches which inosculate with arms of the Biturni and Mahanuddi. The entire length of its course is about 330 m.

BRAMLEY, a parish in the co. of Southampton, 4½ m. N of Basingstoke, through which the London and Southampton railroad passes. Area 2,350 acres. Pop. 428.—Also a p. in Surrey, 3 m. S by E of Guildford, intersected by the Arun and Wey canal. Area 4,420 acres. Pop. 970.—Also a chapelry in the p. of St. Peter, within the liberty of Leeds, W. R. of Yorkshire, 4 m. WNW of Leeds. Area 2,400 acres. Pop. 4,467. The inhabitants are chiefly employed in the manufacture of woollen cloths. Excellent stone and slate are quarried here.
BRAMLEY AND GRANGE, a township in the p. of Braithwell, W. R. of Yorkshire, 10 m. E by N of Sheffield. Area 980 acres. Pop. 353.

BRAMOIS, or Bramis, a village of Switzerland, in the cant. of Valais, and 2 m. ESE of Sion, on the r. bank of the Borgine, 13 m. above the confluence of that river with the Rhone. In the vicinity is the famous hermitage of Longeborne.
BRAMON, an island of Sweden, in the gulf of Bothnia, separated by a narrow channel from the coast of Hernösand.
BRAMPFORD-SPEKE, a parish in the co. of Devon, 4 m. N of Exeter, intersected by the Exeter and Bristol railway. Area 670 acres. Pop. 393.

BRAMPTON, a parish and town in Cumberland, 311 m. N by W of London, and 10 m. E by N of Carlisle, intersected by the Carlisle and Newcastle railroad, which passes 11 m. S of the town. The p. comprises the townships of Easeby and Naworthcastle. Area of the whole, 16,970 acres. Pop. 3,304. —The town of B.—which is by Camden supposed to be on the site of the Roman station Brementuracum -stands in a deep and narrow vale near the junction of the rivers Irthing and Gelt. It is long, irregularly built, and contains few modern houses. The inhabitants are chiefly employed in the manufacture of ginghams and checks, and in two extensive breweries in the vicinity. Corn is largely sold here on marketdays. B. is one of the polling-places for the members for East Cumberland.—About 2 m. to the S of B. is Naworth-castle, the seat of the earls of Carlisle.—On the W declivity of a range of mountains extending from B. to Brough in Westmoreland, a distance of 40 m., a remarkably violent wind occasionally prevails, blowing with tremendous violence down the W slope of the mountain, and extending 2 or 3 m. over the plain at the base. It is termed the 'helm wind;' and its presence is indicated by a belt of clouds, denominated the 'helm bar,' which rests in front of the mountain, 3 or 4 m. W of its summit, and apparently at an equal elevation, remaining immoveable during 24 or even 36 hours, and collecting or attracting to itself all the light clouds which approach it. As long as this bar continues unbroken, the wind blows with unceasing fury, not in gusts, like other storms, but with continued pressure. This wind extends only as far as the spot where the bar is vertical, or immediately overhead; while at the distance of a mile farther W, as well as to the E of the summit of the mountain, it is not unfrequently almost a perfect calm.

BRAMPTON, a parish in the co. of Derby, 3 m. W by N of Chesterfield. Area 8,820 acres. Pop. 3,937. Coal and iron-ore are found in this parish, and pottery-ware is manufactured to a considerable Also a parish in Huntingdonshire, 15 m. SW of Huntingdon. Area 3,640 acres. Pop. 1,164.—Also a township in the p. of Torksey, co. of Lincoln, 75 m. SE of Gainsborough, on the E bank of the river Trent, over which there is a ferry here. Area 790 acres. Pop. 130.-Also a parish in the co. of Norfolk, 2 m. SE of Aylsham, intersected by the river Bure. Area 530 acres. Pop. 263.—Also a parish in the co. of Northampton, 31 m. E of Market- Harborough. Area 2,290 acres. Pop. 104.—Also a parish in Suffolk, 41 m. NE of Halesworth. Area 1,800 acres. Pop. 322.
BRAMPTON (ABBOT'S), a parish in the co. of Hereford, 2 m. N of Ross, on the E bank of the Wye. Area 1,600 acres. Pop. 197.

BRAMPTON-BRYAN, a parish, partly in the co. of Radnor, and partly in the co. of Hereford, 5 m. E of Knighton, on the S bank of the river Teme. Area 3,190 acres. Pop. 419. The village is of considerable antiquity, having been established by the Norman Bryan de Brampton, of whose castle there are still some remains. This fortress, after being twice besieged, was destroyed by the royalists in the parliamentary war.
BRAMPTON-CHAPEL, a parish in the co. of Northampton, 42 m. NNW of Northampton. Area 1,330 acres. Pop. 229.
BRAMPTON-CHURCH, or Brampton Magna, a parish in the co. of Northampton, 4 m. NW by N of Northampton. Area 1,100 acres. Pop. 169.
BRAMPTON (LITTLE), a joint township with Rod and Nash, p. of Presteigne, co. of Hereford, 3½ m. N of Kington. Pop. 162.
BRAMPTON-EN-LE-MORTHEN, a township in the p. of Treeton, W. R. of Yorkshire, 5 m. SE of Rotherham. Area 1,090 acres. Pop. 139.
BRAMS, a promontory of Dutch Guayana, forming the E side of the estuary of the Surinam, in N lat. 5° 55', and W long. 55° 10'.
